title: Give it a role

spec.title: Give it a role
spec.notes_for_generator: Role + context + task. The lesson is that output quality improves dramatically when you assign a specific role before the task. Show the before/after — same task without a role vs with a role. Guided: give the formula (Role / Context / Task) with a worked example, then let them try it on their own scenario.

content:
SUBJECT: AI Gym Day 8: One line that changes everything

You've probably noticed that sometimes an LLM gives you something generic and flat, and sometimes it gives you something that actually sounds considered. The difference is often one sentence you wrote at the start.

TODAY'S WORKOUT
───────────────
✍️ CREATION | 🟢 WARM-UP | ⏱ 10–15 min

THE BRIEF:
You need to draft a short piece of internal communication — a message to a team that's been through a period of change and needs a clear, human update on what's coming next. The content matters, but so does the tone. Today's focus is on how giving the LLM a specific role before the task changes what comes back.

YOUR REPS:

1. **The baseline.** First, ask the AI to draft a short team update (4–6 sentences) about an upcoming change — pick one from your own work context, or invent something plausible (a new process, a team restructure, a shift in priorities). No role assigned. Just the task. Notice what comes back — the tone, the word choices, the level of warmth or formality.

2. **Now add the role.** Use this formula: **Role / Context / Task.** Here's how it works in practice:

   > *"You are an experienced people manager known for clear, honest communication. Your team of eight has just been through a difficult restructure. Write a 4–6 sentence update telling them what happens next — focusing on clarity and reassurance without being patronising."*

   That's the model. Now write your own version using the same formula — with your specific role, your context, and your task. The role should be a real person type, not a vague one ("an expert" won't do much — "a senior HR business partner with a background in change management" will).

3. **Compare and reflect.** Put the two outputs side by side. Identify one specific sentence in the second version that would not have appeared in the first. What did the role instruction actually change — tone, vocabulary, structure, assumptions about the reader? If the two outputs are nearly identical, tighten the role description and run it again.

SUCCESS CRITERIA:
Your output is good if:
• The second draft has a noticeably different tone or register than the first — not just slightly reworded
• You can point to at least one specific phrase that reflects the assigned role's perspective or expertise
• Someone reading the second version could reasonably infer what kind of person wrote it, even without seeing your prompt

💡 COACHING NOTE:
Assigning a role works because it shifts the LLM's frame of reference before it starts generating — it's not a style instruction, it's a perspective instruction. When you tell the model *who* is speaking, it brings in assumptions about what that person knows, what they care about, and how they'd phrase things. The more specific the role, the more those assumptions work in your favour. This principle transfers anywhere you need output with a consistent voice — customer communications, feedback frameworks, job descriptions, strategic narratives.

──────────
Use only material you are allowed to share with your chosen LLM. Anonymise anything sensitive.
Share the programme: https://harrysharman.com/projects/ai-habit/
Found this useful? You can leave a tip: https://ko-fi.com/theaihabit
review_notes: Clarified that AI Habit focuses on chat-based large language models (LLMs), while using AI as public shorthand.
Kept exercises model-agnostic and usable with any text-based LLM or approved work tool.
Reworked research/reasoning language to avoid over-trusting model confidence or hidden chain-of-thought.
Reframed AI Habit as a grounded free 14-day starter block with a paid continuation for deeper 60-90 day habit-building, rather than a validation experiment.

Positioned the paid continuation as The 90-Day AI Habit: a structured applied-LLM programme covering writing/editing, strategy/decision-making, research/assessment, management/delegation, personal productivity, and practical outputs.
Simplified the 90-day commercial route: join individually, bring a small group for a discounted group subscription, or use the workplace launch pack to get an employer to pay.
Added ethical upgrade nudges to Days 3, 7, and 10: endowed progress, career identity, and momentum protection for The 90-Day AI Habit.
Mechanical repair pass: normalised workout badge line after scheduler verification.
